perturbation.original_text: Excluded Information . The Confidential Information shall not include information that (i) is or becomes publicly available other than as a result of acts by the Receiving Party or its Representatives in breach of the terms of this Agreement, (ii) is in the Receiving Party’s possession or the possession of any of its Representatives prior to disclosure by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or is independently derived by the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives without the aid, application or use of the Confidential Information, (iii) is disclosed to the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives by a third party on a non-confidential basis, or (iv) subject to compliance with paragraph 6 below, the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives is advised by counsel that it is required to be disclosed by applicable law, regulation or legal process.
perturbation.changed_text: Excluded Information . The Confidential Information shall not include information that (i) is or becomes publicly available regardless of how it became public, (ii) is in the Receiving Party’s possession or the possession of any of its Representatives prior to disclosure by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or is independently derived by the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives without the aid, application or use of the Confidential Information, (iii) is disclosed to the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives by a third party on a non-confidential basis, or (iv) subject to compliance with paragraph 6 below, the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives is advised by counsel that it is required to be disclosed by applicable law, regulation or legal process.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause (i) in Section 2 removes the condition that information becoming publicly available must not be 'a result of acts by the Receiving Party or its Representatives in breach of the terms of this Agreement'. Now, even if the Receiving Party breaches the agreement and makes the information public, it's no longer considered Confidential Information. This directly contradicts Section 3, which requires the Receiving Party to keep the Confidential Information confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Use and Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information provided to it by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. The Receiving Party shall keep the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible 1 Transaction and who agree to keep such information confidential. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 2 to state that Confidential Information does *not* include information that becomes publicly available regardless of how it became public. This contradicts Section 3, which states that the Receiving Party shall keep the Confidential Information confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information. The change in Section 2 allows the Receiving Party to disclose confidential information by simply making it public, which directly violates the non-disclosure requirement in Section 3. This fits the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' as it creates a conflict between the definition of confidential information and the obligation to keep it confidential.

perturbation.original_text: Use and Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information provided to it by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. The Receiving Party shall keep the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible 1 Transaction and who agree to keep such information confidential. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Use and Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information provided to it by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. The Receiving Party shall keep the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for any purpose and who agree to keep such information confidential. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 3, changing 'evaluating a possible Transaction' to 'any purpose' in exception (ii) regarding disclosure to Representatives creates an in-text contradiction. Initially, Representatives could only receive confidential information for the Transaction. Now, they can receive it for 'any purpose.' This conflicts with the overall intention of the agreement, specifically the first sentence of Section 3 which restricts the Receiving Party to using the information solely for evaluating the Transaction. Now Representatives can use it for any purpose, which contradicts the Receiving Party's restriction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Use and Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information provided to it by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. The Receiving Party shall keep the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for any purpose and who agree to keep such information confidential. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the scope of permissible use of confidential information by the Receiving Party's Representatives. Originally, they could only access and use the information for 'evaluating a possible Transaction,' aligning with the Receiving Party's overall restriction. The changed text allows Representatives to use the information for 'any purpose,' which directly contradicts the first sentence of Section 3 and the contradicted text which states that the Receiving Party and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. This creates a clear in-text contradiction, as the Representatives' permitted use is no longer limited to the Transaction.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Confidential Information . Promptly upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return all copies of the Confidential Information provided by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party to the Disclosing Party, and all other Confidential Information (including, without limitation, all notes, summaries, analysis, compilations, studies, interpretations and memoranda based thereon or extracts, copies and other reproductions thereof or other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect such Confidential Information) shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain (subject to the terms of this Agreement) copies of the Confidential Information to the extent that such retention is required by applicable law or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Return of Confidential Information . Promptly upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return some copies of the Confidential Information provided by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party to the Disclosing Party, and all other Confidential Information (including, without limitation, all notes, summaries, analysis, compilations, studies, interpretations and memoranda based thereon or extracts, copies and other reproductions thereof or other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect such Confidential Information) shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain (subject to the terms of this Agreement) copies of the Confidential Information to the extent that such retention is required by applicable law or regulation.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 5, changing 'all copies' to 'some copies' when referring to the return of Confidential Information upon request creates an internal contradiction. The clause initially mandates the return of all copies, while the modified version only requires returning a portion. This creates an ambiguity as to the definition of how much is 'some' of the total. This also creates the issue of what happens to the remaining 'copies' if they are not being destoryed or returned, which contradicts the use of Confidential Information specified in Section 3
perturbation.contradicted_text: Use and Non-Disclosure of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall use the Confidential Information provided to it by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. The Receiving Party shall keep the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party confidential and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party to which the Disclosing Party gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible 1 Transaction and who agree to keep such information confidential. Each Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5 alters 'all copies' to 'some copies' regarding the return of Confidential Information, directly contradicting the requirement for confidentiality and non-disclosure specified in the Contradicted Text (Section 3). Section 3 requires keeping 'the Confidential Information provided to it by the Disclosing Party confidential', while changing 'all' to 'some' in Section 5 introduces an ambiguity about what happens to the remaining copies. These unreturned copies are no longer explicitly covered by the destruction clause in Section 5, and their use is not clearly defined, creating a direct conflict with Section 3's confidentiality requirements. Because this means that the copies that are not returned create uncertainty in regards to the agreements requirement to use of Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction, this leads to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Standstill . For a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, each Party agrees that it shall not, and that it shall cause is affiliates and subsidiaries who have received the other Party’s Confidential Information, or who are aware of the possibility of the Transaction, not to, directly or indirectly, unless specifically invited in advance by the other Party’s Board of Directors: (i) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or sell or otherwise dispose of, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) of any of the other Party’s assets or businesses or any securities issued by such other Party, or any rights or options to acquire such ownership (including from a third party), (ii) seek or propose to influence or control of the other Party’s management or policies (or request permission to do so), or (iii) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Standstill . For a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, each Party agrees that it shall not, and that it shall cause is affiliates and subsidiaries who have received the other Party’s Confidential Information, or who are aware of the possibility of the Transaction, not to, directly or indirectly, unless generally invited in advance by the other Party’s Board of Directors: (i) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or sell or otherwise dispose of, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) of any of the other Party’s assets or businesses or any securities issued by such other Party, or any rights or options to acquire such ownership (including from a third party), (ii) seek or propose to influence or control of the other Party’s management or policies (or request permission to do so), or (iii) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'specifically invited' to 'generally invited' in Section 10 creates an in-text contradiction. Originally, each action required a specific invitation. Now, a general invitation could be interpreted as covering multiple actions, or all actions. This is contradicting the idea that the two Parties should not acquire shares or influence the company. A 'general invitation' could be interepreted as them agreeing to the transaction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Standstill . For a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, each Party agrees that it shall not, and that it shall cause is affiliates and subsidiaries who have received the other Party’s Confidential Information, or who are aware of the possibility of the Transaction, not to, directly or indirectly, unless generally invited in advance by the other Party’s Board of Directors: (i) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or sell or otherwise dispose of, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) of any of the other Party’s assets or businesses or any securities issued by such other Party, or any rights or options to acquire such ownership (including from a third party), (ii) seek or propose to influence or control of the other Party’s management or policies (or request permission to do so), or (iii) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'specifically invited' to 'generally invited' introduces ambiguity into the standstill provision. Previously, any action that would violate the standstill (acquiring shares, influencing management, etc.) required a specific invitation from the other party's board. Now, a general invitation could be interpreted as blanket permission for multiple or all such actions. This contradicts the intent of the standstill agreement, which is to prevent such actions unless explicitly and individually approved. The contradicted text is the exact same Standstill clause.

perturbation.original_text: Remedies . Each Party acknowledges that in the event of any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the other Party could not be made whole by monetary damages. Accordingly, each Party, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity, shall be entitled to an injunction to prevent breaches of this Agreement, and to an order compelling specific performance of this Agreement. A breaching Party shall reimburse the other Party for all reasonable costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, incurred by the other Party in the event it successfully enforces the obligations of the breaching Party or its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Remedies . Each Party acknowledges that in the event of any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the other Party could potentially be made whole by monetary damages. Accordingly, each Party, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity, shall be entitled to an injunction to prevent breaches of this Agreement, and to an order compelling specific performance of this Agreement. A breaching Party shall reimburse the other Party for all reasonable costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, incurred by the other Party in the event it successfully enforces the obligations of the breaching Party or its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'could not be made whole' to 'could potentially be made whole' in Section 12 introduces ambiguity. This directly contradicts the purpose of seeking an injunction and specific performance. If monetary damages could potentially suffice, the need for equitable remedies is undermined, which calls into question if they should be entitled to an injunction. If there are monetary damages available, then those could be used instead of the injunction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies . Each Party acknowledges that in the event of any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the other Party could potentially be made whole by monetary damages. Accordingly, each Party, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity, shall be entitled to an injunction to prevent breaches of this Agreement, and to an order compelling specific performance of this Agreement. A breaching Party shall reimburse the other Party for all reasonable costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, incurred by the other Party in the event it successfully enforces the obligations of the breaching Party or its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that monetary damages 'could not be made whole' in the event of a breach. The changed text alters this to 'could potentially be made whole,' directly contradicting the basis for seeking equitable remedies like injunctions and specific performance. If monetary damages are potentially sufficient, the need for these equitable remedies is undermined, creating a contradiction because the contract asserts entitlement to both. The contradicted text highlights that 'could potentially be made whole' weakens the argument for an injunction
